narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 67.5% chance that you would not pass it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st, which was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signaled lower early academic performance compared to peers. Your Year 3 decile of 2nd also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent performance. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward success and highlighting it as one of your standout achievements relative to averages. Your middle family income bracket played a smaller role in tipping toward the failure prediction, while your LSAT score of 32—below the average of 36.7—had a minor similar effect. Being enrolled full-time, which is the norm for most students at 92.7%, offered a tiny boost toward passing but wasn't enough to shift the balance. Compared to typical applicants, your early deciles were well below averages around the 5th or 6th, which weighed heavily against you despite your stronger GPA. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's just how these key academic markers stacked up in the model's assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your early-grade deciles through additional undergrad or prep courses if possible, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for diverse students like yourself. Many have turned similar profiles around with focused improvement—your GPA shows you have the foundation to do it. Keep pushing; law schools value resilience, and reapplying with enhanced metrics could change the outcome next time. We're rooting for you.
